perturbation.original_text: Heritage shall bear all costs, fees and out-of-pocket expenses associated with any Recall Action which results from (i) Heritage's or Jasper's negligence or willful misconduct, (ii) Heritage's or Jasper's failure to comply with Product Specifications or the Post Holdings Quality Expectations Manual set forth on Schedule A, (iii) any breach of this Agreement by Heritage or (iv) ingredients or packaging materials purchased by Heritage or Jasper. In all other cases, Premier shall bear all costs associated with any Recall Action.
perturbation.changed_text: Heritage shall bear all costs, fees and out-of-pocket expenses associated with any Recall Action which results from (i) Heritage's or Jasper's negligence or willful misconduct, (ii) Heritage's or Jasper's failure to comply with Product Specifications or the Post Holdings Quality Expectations Manual set forth on Schedule A, (iii) any breach of this Agreement by Heritage or (iv) ingredients or packaging materials purchased by Heritage or Jasper. In all other cases, Premier shall bear all costs associated with any Weekly Production Report. 2. Monthly Inventory Reports at Supplier's end of fiscal month to include a. Inventory on hand, b. Inventory on hold, and c. Inventory adjusted. 3. Monthly Purchase Order receipt report - at the end of Supplier's fiscal month
perturbation.explanation: Moving the portion regarding who bears the costs of a recall action into the section describing Weekly Production Reports will severely reduce its ability to be seen and acted upon, especially the part that says Premier shall bear all costs associated with any Recall Action. This is legally dangerous.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. § 350l addresses hazard analysis and critical control points (HACCP) systems for seafood. By obscuring the financial responsibilities for recall actions, the agreement potentially hinders the prompt and effective execution of recalls, which are crucial for compliance with HACCP principles and the protection of public health. This obfuscation could lead to delays in addressing potential hazards, thus contradicting the intent of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 21 - FOOD AND DRUGS 21 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 21 - FOOD AND DRUGS CHAPTER 9 - FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC ACT SUBCHAPTER IV - FOOD Sec. 350l - Mandatory recall authority From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §350 l . Mandatory recall authority (a) Voluntary procedures If the Secretary determines, based on information gathered through the reportable food registry under section 350f of this title or through any other means, that there is a reasonable probability that an article of food (other than infant formula) is adulterated under section 342 of this title or misbranded under section 343(w) of this title and the use of or exposure to such article will cause serious adverse health consequences or death to humans or animals, the Secretary shall provide the responsible party (as defined in section 350f of this title) with an opportunity to cease distribution and recall such article. (b) Prehearing order to cease distribution and give notice (1) In general If the responsible party refuses to or does not voluntarily cease distribution or recall such article within the time and in the manner prescribed by the Secretary (if so prescribed), the Secretary may, by order require, as the Secretary deems necessary, such person to— (A) immediately cease distribution of such article; and (B) as applicable, immediately notify all persons— (i) manufacturing, processing, packing, transporting, distributing, receiving, holding, or importing and selling such article; and (ii) to which such article has been distributed, transported, or sold, to immediately cease distribution of such article. 1 (2) Required additional information (A) In general If an article of food covered by a recall order issued under paragraph (1)(B) has been distributed to a warehouse-based third party logistics provider without providing such provider sufficient information to know or reasonably determine the precise identity of the article of food covered by a recall order that is in its possession, the notice provided by the responsible party subject to the order issued under paragraph (1)(B) shall include such information as is necessary for the warehouse-based third party logistics provider to identify the food. (B) R

perturbation.justification: The original contract clearly delineates the financial responsibilities for recall actions, stating who bears the costs under specific circumstances. The changed text moves the part regarding financial responsibility for recall action, specifically mentioning Premier bearing all costs, into the section describing Weekly Production Reports. This constitutes a structural flaw that obscures a legally significant obligation. The Law Explanation states that obscuring these financial responsibilities can hinder the effective execution of recalls, which is crucial for compliance. Snippet 1, 21 U.S.C. § 350l, discusses mandatory recall authority, implying the importance of clear procedures. The change makes it difficult to easily locate and act upon this critical clause, creating a legal contradiction as defined in the Structural Flaws category.

perturbation.original_text: Heritage agrees to provide to Premier each FDA Form 483 and any related Establishment Inspection Report ("EIR") that is received from the FDA by Heritage or Jasper, along with any response provided to the regulatory authority by Heritage or Jasper, as long as this Agreement is in effect. Heritage agrees to do so within [***] of Heritage or Jasper receiving the Report or of sending the response as appropriate. If Heritage wishes to redact any material from any EIR, it shall indicate that deletion by use of the following note where each redaction occurs: "REDACTED MATERIAL". Heritage agrees that it will not redact any information on an EIR that directly relates to any aspect of its manufacturing of Products for Premier.
perturbation.changed_text: Heritage agrees to provide to Premier each FDA Form 483 and any related Establishment Inspection Report ("EIR") that is received from the FDA by Heritage or Jasper, along with any response provided to the regulatory authority by Heritage or Jasper, as long as this Agreement is in effect. Heritage agrees to do so within [***] of Heritage or Jasper receiving the Report or of sending the response as appropriate. If Heritage wishes to redact any material from any EIR, it shall indicate that deletion by use of the following note where each redaction occurs: "REDACTED MATERIAL". Heritage agrees that it will not redact any information on an EIR that directly relates to any aspect of its manufacturing of Products for Premier.8. RISK OF LOSS AND INSURANCE (a) Title to the Products shall be in and remain with Premier from the date Products are delivered to a carrier pursuant to Premier's instructions for delivery to Premier. Heritage shall bear the risk of loss to the Products until the Products are delivered to such carrier for delivery to Premier as set forth herein. Risk of loss to the Products shall also be with Heritage during shipment between the Heritage Facilities pursuant to Section 2.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the portion regarding providing FDA Form 483 reports to the Risk of Loss and Insurance Section reduces the ability to be seen and acted upon.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 111.535 requires documentation of quality control operations, including corrective actions. By placing the disclosure of FDA inspection reports within the risk of loss and insurance section, the agreement diminishes the emphasis on proactive quality control and transparency, potentially hindering the identification and implementation of necessary corrective actions as required by 21 CFR § 111.535. This could lead to non-compliance and increased risk of unsafe products.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that Heritage will provide Premier with FDA Form 483 and EIR reports, including responses to regulatory authorities. The modified text moves this obligation into the 'Risk of Loss and Insurance' section. This constitutes a structural flaw. The LLM explanation asserts that this placement diminishes the emphasis on proactive quality control and transparency, potentially hindering the identification and implementation of necessary corrective actions as required by 21 CFR § 111.535. Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 111.535, stating it concerns records that must be made and kept under subpart N. By relocating the obligation to disclose FDA reports to an unrelated section, the agreement weakens its visibility and enforceability, potentially undermining compliance with regulations concerning quality control documentation.
